title: A Look at the Gnome Pack #1 - Promo / Card pack from Grey Gnome Games
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Y8YjZOGrhJI

Daniel (@dungeondive) opens *Gnome Pack #1*, a Jason Glover promo bundle from Grey Gnome Games that drops new cards into seven of his existing games and one tantalising standalone Oracle deck.

The big ticket is *Iron Helm*: twenty new cards introducing race/class character creation (eight ancestries — half folk, merfolk, half-orc, dwarf, gnome, human, crawling, wood elf — paired with eight classes via card-flip combinatorics, à la Tin Helm), plus tracking cards for every stat, plastic stat tokens, and a Star Sign deck of twelve double-sided astrological bonuses. *Tin Helm* gets new dungeon locations (Lost Tomb, Endless Pit, Crawlway), two new race/class cards (swine folk/Seer, construct/Brute), and two new trappings. *DustRunner* gets five new cards including a difficulty-up tracker and new modifications/encounters. *Gate/Gates* gets seven all-new cards including three wave-bosses and a new location. *Desolate* receives a danger deck. A *Stone Fire* dodo bird card slips into that game's wilderness deck.

The Oracle deck — six double-sided cards — is the surprise: d6 roll, plot action, yes/no oracle, creature/item/place, plot character, descriptor, plus encounter icons and dungeon-piece prompts. Effectively a complete solo-RPG generator in a handful of cards. Daniel calls it a clever Trojan horse: bundling promos for games you don't own makes you want them.

----

Which is the better promo design — fresh content for games you already love, or a Trojan horse that tempts you toward games you don't yet own?
